steps.title: Identify what needs to be joined
steps.content: The blank appears between two independent clauses: “Okinaka doesn't make such decisions single-handedly” and “All historical designations must be approved by a group of nine other experts.” The transition however can appear at the end of the first sentence when it is set off with punctuation, as in IC, however. IC.

steps.title: Eliminate faulty boundaries and confirm the correct pattern
steps.content:
• Choice A — however, all: Places however between the two independent clauses with only commas, creating a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — however and all: Adds and after however, making the transition grammatically incoherent. ✗
  • Choice C — however all: Does not provide the punctuation needed after however, so the two clauses are not correctly separated. ✗
  • Choice D — however. All: Sets off however at the end of the first sentence, then starts the next independent clause as a new sentence. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
